Beyond Render and Fly: Zeabur Is Worth a Look

Since Heroku killed its free tier, every cloud platform has rolled out free offers to capture the migrants. Render.com and Fly.io tend to be the names you see most, but there's another worth knowing about — Zeabur. So this post is the quick "Zeabur exists, here's how it works" tour.
In short, Zeabur is a deployment platform like Render.com and Fly.io — push code, get a running service. What sets it apart is the pitch:
. As close to "one-click" deploy as they can get, so you spend less time on infra and more time on your actual project.
A lot of new devs see "deploy platform with English docs" and panic. Heads up — Zeabur has Chinese docs too:
Zeabur's founders are Mandarin speakers, so the docs ship in multiple languages. Quick links:

Heads up — this post was written 2023/04/01, and Zeabur was still under active development. They've already published pricing, and the free tier was already enough for normal use; the paid tiers were reasonable too:
OK enough intro — time to actually deploy something.
You need to agree to the Terms of Service — click "Terms of service":
Next screen warns you not to do certain things on their platform (you have to wait 10 seconds):
If you're not planning anything dodgy, hit "I Will not deploy any of that". Now you can sign up via GitHub:
It's a standard GitHub OAuth flow — I'll skip the details.
After login you land on the Zeabur dashboard.
On first login the dashboard is mostly empty — click "Create Project":
Name the project — I named mine "example-express":
Click into the project from the dashboard:
To deploy, find the Service section and click "Add Service":
Pick how you want to deploy — GitHub or Marketplace. I'll use GitHub:
), name the Service whatever, then hit "Deploy":
Status shows up in the dashboard as it deploys:
Zeabur auto-detects what kind of project you're deploying — here it picked "Node.js" for us. Once you see "RUNNING", the deploy is done. (And it's fast.)
Scroll down to customize the domain — I went with "2022-express-example". Click "Save":
The custom domain shows up under Deployments:
Click it and your project opens — clean and simple.

That's the basic flow. There's plenty more — custom Dockerfile deploys, Marketplace services, and so on — all in the Zeabur docs. The point of this post is just: if you're shopping for an alternative to Render and Fly, Zeabur's another option.
By the way — you can switch the dashboard UI to Chinese. Click your avatar → "Settings":
Zeabur's still in active development, so some UI bits and docs may shift. If you've used Zeabur and want to help improve the docs, the repo's open:
For a new platform, the docs are surprisingly thorough and easy to follow. I'm looking forward to seeing where Zeabur goes — hopefully this post puts it on a few more people's radar.
If you hit issues using Zeabur, they also have a Discord community:

After Heroku killed its free tier, every cloud platform rushed in with free offerings to scoop up those users. Render.com and Fly.io are the usual recommendations, but there's another one worth knowing about — Zeabur, a deployment platform that bills itself on simplicity and "one-click" deploys. This post walks through signing up, deploying an Express app from GitHub, and customizing the domain.
